title: How the Web Works
description: The core concepts behind every website you visit
congratsText: You now understand how the web works end to end!

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Explain the difference between the Internet and the Web
  • Describe the client-server model and the role of each side
  • Read and understand the parts of a URL
  • Explain what DNS does and why it matters
  • Describe what HTTP and HTTPS are and why HTTPS is preferred
  • Trace the full request-response cycle from browser to server and back
  • Understand the roles of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript in a web page
  • Describe what a web browser actually does under the hood
Why this matters: Every time you open a website, a precise sequence of events happens in milliseconds. Understanding this sequence helps you think like a web developer — and it explains why things go wrong when they do.

sections.title: The Internet vs The Web

quiz.question: What is the Internet?
options: The global network of cables, routers, and devices
A single website hosted on one computer somewhere
The browser software you use to visit websites here
The company that provides your internet connection

sections.lesson:
The Internet vs The Web
These two terms are often used interchangeably, but they are different things.
The Internet
  • The physical infrastructure
  • Cables, routers, satellites
  • Connects billions of devices
  • Carries all kinds of data (email, calls, streaming, web)
The Web
  • A service that runs on top of the Internet
  • Web pages, links, and media
  • Accessed using a browser
  • Uses the HTTP/HTTPS protocol
Analogy: The Internet is like the road network — the physical infrastructure. The Web is like one of the services that uses those roads, similar to how courier delivery uses roads but is not the same as the roads themselves.
Other services that use the Internet include email, online gaming, video calls, and file transfer — none of which are the Web, but all of which depend on the Internet.

sections.title: Client & Server

quiz.question: What is the role of a client and a server?
options: The client sends requests; the server responds to them
The server asks the client what pages to display first
Both the client and server store the website HTML files
The client and server always run on the same machine

sections.lesson:
Client & Server
The web is built on a simple two-party model: a client that asks for things, and a server that provides them.
Client
  • Your browser (Chrome, Firefox…)
  • Makes requests for web pages
  • Receives and displays the response
  • Runs on your device
Server
  • A computer in a data center
  • Stores and serves web files
  • Listens for incoming requests
  • Sends back the requested resource
The interaction is always initiated by the client:
  1. You type a URL and press Enter
  2. Your browser (client) sends a request to the server
  3. The server finds the requested file
  4. The server sends it back to the client
  5. Your browser displays the page
A single server can handle millions of clients simultaneously. And a single client can talk to many different servers — one for every website you visit.

sections.title: What is a URL?

quiz.question: What is a URL?
options: The local file path to the HTML file on your computer
A unique address that identifies a resource on the web
The name of the browser software used to view the page
The IP address of the user who is currently visiting

sections.lesson:
What is a URL?
A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is the unique address of a resource on the web. Every web page, image, video, or file has one.
https://www.example.com:443/about/team?id=5#contact │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─ Fragment (section anchor) │ │ │ │ └──── Query string (parameters) │ │ │ └──────────────── Path (specific page/file) │ │ └──────────────────── Port (optional, 443 = HTTPS) │ └──────────────────────────────────── Domain name └──────────────────────────────────────────── Protocol (HTTPS)
Breaking it down:
  • Protocol — https:// — how data is transferred (HTTP or HTTPS)
  • Domain — www.example.com — human-readable name of the server
  • Path — /about/team — which specific page or file on that server
  • Query string — ?id=5 — extra data sent to the server (e.g. search terms)
  • Fragment — #contact — jumps to a specific section on the page
Most URLs you use daily only have the protocol, domain, and path. The query string and fragment are optional.

sections.title: DNS — The Web's Phone Book

quiz.question: What does the Domain Name System (DNS) do?
options: It translates domain names into IP addresses for routing
It encrypts web traffic so that nobody can read it at all
It stores copies of websites to speed up loading times
It controls which users are allowed to access a website

sections.lesson:
DNS — The Web's Phone Book
Computers communicate using IP addresses — numerical identifiers like 142.250.190.46. Humans use domain names like google.com. DNS bridges the gap.
The DNS lookup process:
  1. You type www.google.com in your browser
  2. Your browser checks its local cache — has it looked this up before?
  3. If not, it asks a DNS resolver (usually your ISP's server)
  4. The resolver finds the IP address: 142.250.190.46
  5. Your browser now knows where to send the request
Analogy: DNS is like a phone book. You look up "Google" to find the actual phone number. Without DNS, you would have to memorize a string of numbers for every website you visit.
DNS is also why:
  • A website can move to a new server without changing its domain name
  • One domain can point to multiple servers for load balancing
  • You can block websites by manipulating DNS at the router level

sections.title: HTTP & HTTPS

quiz.question: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
options: HTTPS encrypts data; HTTP sends it in plain text only
HTTP is faster; HTTPS only works on mobile devices
HTTPS requires a paid browser plugin to even function
HTTP is newer and more secure than HTTPS always here

sections.lesson:
HTTP & HTTPS
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the set of rules that governs how web data is requested and sent between clients and servers. It is the language of the web.
HTTP Methods — the most common:
  • GET — retrieve a resource (loading a page, fetching data)
  • POST — send data to the server (submitting a form, logging in)
  • PUT / PATCH — update existing data on the server
  • DELETE — remove a resource from the server
HTTPS = HTTP + Encryption
HTTP
  • Data sent in plain text
  • Anyone between you and the server can read it
  • No padlock in the browser bar
  • Unsafe for passwords or payments
HTTPS
  • Data is encrypted with TLS
  • Interceptors see scrambled data only
  • Padlock icon in the browser bar
  • Required for login, payments, forms
Modern browsers warn users when a site uses HTTP instead of HTTPS, and search engines rank HTTPS sites higher.

sections.title: The Request–Response Cycle

quiz.question: What is the correct order of the request-response cycle?
options: Client → DNS → Server → Browser renders the response
Server → DNS → Client → Server sends the response
DNS → Client → Server → Client forwards to another server
Browser → Server → DNS → Client renders the page

sections.lesson:
The Request–Response Cycle
Every page load is a conversation between your browser and a server. Here is the full sequence:
  1. You enter a URL — e.g. https://www.example.com/about
  2. DNS lookup — browser translates example.com to an IP address
  3. TCP connection — browser establishes a connection to the server
  4. TLS handshake — (HTTPS only) browser and server agree on encryption
  5. HTTP Request sent — browser asks: GET /about HTTP/1.1
  6. Server processes — finds the file or runs code to build the response
  7. HTTP Response sent — server replies with a status code + the content
  8. Browser renders — parses HTML, loads CSS and JS, displays the page
HTTP Status Codes — what the server replies with:
  • 200 OK — success, here is the content you requested
  • 301 Moved Permanently — the URL has changed, go to this new one
  • 404 Not Found — the requested page does not exist on this server
  • 500 Internal Server Error — the server crashed trying to handle the request

sections.title: HTML, CSS & JavaScript

quiz.question: What are the roles of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript?
options: HTML adds style; CSS defines structure; JS handles logic
HTML defines structure; CSS adds style; JS adds behavior
CSS defines structure; HTML adds color; JS renders HTML
JS builds the page; HTML handles clicks; CSS sends data

sections.lesson:
HTML, CSS & JavaScript
When the server sends back a web page, it is made of three core technologies. The browser knows how to read all three.
HTML
  • Structure & content
  • Headings, paragraphs, images, links, buttons
  • The skeleton of the page
CSS
  • Style & layout
  • Colors, fonts, spacing, animations
  • The appearance of the page
JavaScript — behavior and interactivity:
  • Responds to user actions (clicks, typing, scrolling)
  • Fetches new data from the server without reloading the page (AJAX)
  • Validates forms, shows/hides content, animates elements
  • Runs in the browser — no page reload needed
A house analogy:
  • HTML = the walls, floors, and rooms (structure)
  • CSS = the paint, furniture, and lighting (style)
  • JavaScript = the electricity and plumbing (what makes it interactive)

sections.title: What Does a Browser Do?

quiz.question: What does a web browser actually do?
options: A browser reads HTML, CSS, JS and renders them visually
A browser stores web pages permanently on your hard drive
A browser is a server that sends web pages to clients
A browser writes HTML code for the web server to run

sections.lesson:
What Does a Web Browser Do?
A web browser is software that retrieves resources from the web, understands HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, and renders them into what you see on screen.
What happens inside the browser after receiving a page:
  1. Parse HTML — reads the HTML and builds the DOM (Document Object Model), a tree of all elements on the page
  2. Parse CSS — reads style rules and calculates how each element should look
  3. Render tree — combines the DOM with CSS to determine the final visual layout
  4. Paint — draws every pixel on the screen
  5. Run JavaScript — executes scripts that may update the DOM or fetch more data
Browsers also handle:
  • Caching — storing copies of files locally to speed up future visits
  • Cookies — small data files that websites use to remember you
  • Security — blocking unsafe sites, enforcing HTTPS, sandboxing JS code
  • Dev Tools — built-in tools for inspecting, debugging, and profiling pages

sections.title: Recap: The Full Journey

quiz.question: What is the correct order of events when you visit a website?
options: DNS lookup → HTTP request → Server → Response → Render
Browser renders → Server → DNS lookup → HTTP response
HTTP request → Browser renders → DNS → Server responds
Server → DNS → Browser renders → HTTP request is sent

sections.lesson:
Recap: The Full Journey of a Web Request
Let's trace everything from start to finish. You type https://www.example.com and press Enter.
  1. DNS Lookup — Browser asks DNS: "What is the IP address of example.com?" → gets 93.184.216.34
  2. TCP + TLS — Browser opens a secure connection to that IP address
  3. HTTP GET Request — Browser asks the server: "Give me the homepage"
  4. Server responds — Status 200 OK + sends back the HTML file
  5. Browser parses HTML — Discovers it needs a CSS file and a JS file
  6. More requests — Browser fetches the CSS, JS, images (each one is a new request)
  7. Render — Browser builds the DOM, applies styles, runs scripts, paints the screen
  8. Page is visible — All in typically under 2 seconds
Key concepts at a glance:
  • Internet — the physical network that carries all data
  • Web — a service on the Internet using HTTP/HTTPS
  • Client — your browser; makes requests
  • Server — a computer in a data center; responds to requests
  • URL — the unique address of a resource
  • DNS — translates domain names to IP addresses
  • HTTP/HTTPS — the rules for how data is requested and sent
  • HTML / CSS / JS — the three building blocks of every web page
  • Browser — parses and renders everything into what you see
